And now, John, the nine minute miracle, my friend. I'll bet you've been called the nine minute miracle before. Have it. Very funny. Good morning. Well, it's better than the three minute miracle. All right. Is anybody mad at me now? Nobody? I'm disappointed. OK, way in the back. All right. You people are sticking around. You've been drinking coffee and your bladders are full. Anybody? Tough. Anybody want to learn how to write a damn good ad in nine minutes? Are you sure? How many people are responsible for the writing or the advertising in your business, whatever it is? Just give me a show of hands. OK, most people. How many, leave your hands up. Leave your hands up. How many of you think you're good at it right now? OK, if you want to look around the room, most nobody. That's where I come in. We have our thing. Yeah, I don't have any unfinished business. We can launch right into it. You reset the time. We are ready to go. Dan still works from this overhead thing. Don't turn it on. But this is old school. When I started out, too, I had the transparencies. And I'd always put the transparencies on upside down and backwards. And the crowd would titter and scream. And I realized that was a great involvement device. I used to do it on purpose after a while, put stuff upside down. This is very old school. I'm even more old school than Dan is, because I work from printed notes. All right, I have a clicker here. All right, the good part about this, and a lot of the questions that I got over the last few days, all came down to a sense of writing. And I want to reinforce the notion that I'm not going to teach you how to write. Writing is a very important thing. Writing is a word that packs a whole lot of emotional baggage that doesn't need to be there. So if you don't want to write, consider yourself a writer, or you're fearful of writing, then think of it as creating, not writing something, but creating a sales message. You never have to put pencil with paper. You never have to sit down with a keyboard. You never have to do any of this stuff. Do you guys all know the quick three-step way to write an ad really fast when you're in trouble? OK, if you know how to sell something to somebody, you sit down and record it, and then you transcribe it. And then have somebody type it out, clean it up. That's not a bad ad. I'm going to teach you how to do a better version here. But that way, your hands never touch a keyboard. So this is not about writing. Step one of this process, how to write a damn good ad in nine minutes, hit the bar, look for trouble. The other little story, it's great that I'm following down with this story telling stuff, because this is dovetails perfectly with that. The reason I talked about the elevator pitch is because it made it vivid in your head. How many people had a breakthrough when they finally understood the elevator pitch idea? OK, good chunk of the room. This is, you don't need to hit a bar. It can be a coffee shop. It can be any place where people gather. But here is the image I want you to have in your mind. You are sitting there minding your own business. Somebody walks up, sits down next to you, and says, not to you, but to the bartender. Says, Joe, give me a double. I just had an awful day. And the bartender says, gee, Ed, why did you have such a bad day as he's pouring him a double shot? And Ed, the guy who sat down next to you, says what his problem was. And interestingly enough, what you offer, your product, your service, is exactly what fixes the problem that Ed has. So what do you say to Ed? Let me make sure I'm not getting ahead of myself here. Yes. This would be step two. So I want you to have this image in mind, because a good ad is what? It's a conversation with somebody. The only difference between a good face-to-face salesman and an ad in print or a video ad online or any kind of sales message is that it's some form of a conversation. Where big corporations go wrong, where entrepreneurs go wrong with their advertising, is they get away from that idea of it being a conversation. It's human-to-human interaction. I like to call it just you and me talking. You ever seen somebody in a corner of a room? There's a lot of movement going on, but there's two people in a corner of a room, and they're sitting, facing each other, and they're slumped forward, and they're talking urgently. That is the conversation you're having with your prospects. The good thing about written ads is that you can multiply that by an infinite number of times. So rather than have a face-to-face with every single possible prospect you can have, you can multiply that by making it a written ad or a video or something like that. So step two. What would you say to your perfect prospect? Let's call him Ed, who doesn't yet know how awesome you are. Think about that for just a second. I love these pregnant pauses from the stage now. There is one hint here. The hint is he doesn't like you yet. Number one mistake rookie marketers make is they think that because you have a product that fixes his problem, he is immediately putty in your hands. There's nothing more he would like to do than to sit there and listen to you babble endlessly about what you do and why it's so great. Take an hour to get to the point because you don't know how to do an elevator chat. Okay. What would you say? You should have this in your mind. We don't have time to go through examples of this, but think about this. What would you say? Would you say I have an elaborate scheme that takes about an hour to explain and I have a $10,000 program that if you buy you probably would understand it at the end of that program. Is that what you say? No. What do you say? Say something along the lines of what? What would be the first words at your mouth? Somebody, anybody? Come on. No, come on. First words at your mouth. Excuse me. I couldn't help overhearing. Now that probably won't make it into your ad, but you understand that that is just code for social lubrication. So you got to keep that in mind. He doesn't like you. He's not ready to hear from you. What would you do if you were sitting in somewhere and a stranger turned to you and started jabbering at you? What's your first instinct? Get the hell out of there. Same with written ads and stuff. That's why the most important part of your ad is going to be your headline, your superscript with the little part above the headline. First things they see, you have split seconds to get his attention and get him into the story. You don't have an infinite amount of time. So what you would say, and all of you should be cogitating on this as I'm talking, what you would say to him, that's step three, that's your headline. Let's say Bob is having a bad day because he's got a website that isn't polling. It used to do really well with organic search and out of competition is eating them alive and he's not making any money. You might turn him to say, excuse me, I couldn't help over here. I had the same problem quite a while ago and I learned to solve it. Now I got four times the number of hits that I'm getting. You start talking the language that he's talking, and then the conversation is hit. Think about that. If he says, I'm not turning hits into sales, for example, if that's how he describes his problem, and you say, wow, I solved that about a year ago and I've been helping about 180,000 people do that through my coaching program, but right now I'm quadrupling sales. Then you turn that around to a how-to headline and you turn that around to a how-to quadruple your sales online even if competition is eating you alive. The key here, and this is one of the things you want to write down, short, sizzling, and specific. And if you have trouble writing headlines, go to the standard fallback headline that starts with how-to. What follows how-to is basically how you are talking to that guy. Are we tracking him? Is this making sense to you? Anybody lost yet? How about that guy from Russia? Are you lost yet? Step four, introduce yourself and establish your credibility. How many people have had to do this face-to-face with a stranger who may be a cold-to-lucwarm prospect, but you've had to establish your credibility in a very, very short period of time? How many people have had to do that face-to-face? Was it easy? It was easy, really? Okay, good for you. It's not easy. For most people, it's very, very hard. Because what do you do wrong? What do most people do wrong when they're trying to establish credibility? They brag. What you guys want to do is bond. A lot of what Dan was talking about involves bonding. The origin myth. My origin myth was great. I mean, it's absolutely true. I was sleeping on a friend's couch, living out of my car. I had this epiphany. Nothing was going to change unless I was going to be the engine of change. And that took me from slackerdom to starting my long process of discovering how to make things happen. So, how is this imaginary conversation going so far in your head with this guy in the bar? Are you starting to progress a little bit? Now, step five in this thing. Imagine that a happy client of yours stops by. He says, what's your name, sir? He says, Doug, how you doing? Man, I can't believe it. I saw you here. That advice you gave me last week? My God, it worked like crazy. I have quadrupled my sales online since I did what you told me to do. That's great. Sorry to interrupt. Doug's the greatest guy in the world. I don't know who you are, but listen to John. Pat your on the bat and walks away. You guys know what that is? The best thing that could happen to you in that face-to-face conversation would be something to come by and do in that in person. When you've got an advertisement or something, you have to make that happen. That's all testimonials are. Third-party endorsement, when you say it, it's bragging. Somebody else says it, it's credibility. Now we start getting to the meat. And this involves shameless teasing with benefits. Anybody weak on the... This is going to look crazy on film. I'm always doing this kind of thing, but I got to look out here. Anybody weak on the idea of features versus benefits? If you've been in Dan's world for more than 10 minutes, you should know all of this. A feature is the heft weight, the clunk value, whatever you have. So the numbers, the physical nature of the product, the things that you do in your service. The benefits are what? What it does for you. Who said that? What was your name, sir? Oh, Mark, how you doing? I can't see. Shameless teasing with benefits. I'm going to read a finished conversation for you guys in a bit. This could come, but I want you to suffer a bit thinking about it in your own stew here. Shameless teasing, does anybody have a problem with shamelessly teasing a prospect with benefits? Do you understand why I use the term shameless teasing? When you explain too much in an ad, what do you do? Start to bore them. If you make it happen in their head, you are creating action. You are starting to spur them on. If you solve everything, there's nothing left for them to do. There are two kinds of bullets, and this refers to, I would call this the bulletizing part, where everybody know what a bullet is or a fascination? It's a little bullet is a dot, and you have a little line of copy that connects usually a feature with a benefit, something like this. There are two kinds of bullets that professional copy writers use. The explanatory bullet, which says, I have what I call the duck worth method that will help you actually do the three things necessary to get a client to do X. And then you list those three things, for example, if he's over 50 years old, it will show him how to X. The other kind of bullet is called a blind bullet. When professional writers write, David, actually it's in the contract of Rudeil, I think, of how many blind bullets versus explanatory bullets you can put in. Do they do that with boardroom? But it's about a 10 to 1, isn't it? About 10 blind bullets to one explanatory bullet. You do the explanatory bullet as part of your what? Credibility. You actually know how to do this. What I'm doing up here, these two sessions that I had, I'm actually showing you how to do something. That's an explanatory. I could have come up here and just teased about it. I know how to make you all rich. If you follow me, I will make you wealthy, happy, and wise. And not tell you how I'm going to do that. But the teasing stuff is where the talent comes in. In the conversation, you might say something like, well, yeah, I mean, I learned how to quadruple my profits online. And I did it in an hour and a half, and it was really great. And before, I just stumbled on this idea, and it was great. And it worked really easy, and I taught my 10-year-old daughter how to do it. And I taught this guy who actually has learning disabilities how to do it. And it was great, and it worked really fast. And these guys are not just quadrupling their profits, but even 10 times and 20 times. But I don't want to bore you with the details. But it works really easy. OK? I'm getting a lot of blank looks. Have I lost anybody? We're zooming through this. In fact, anybody want to try? You can walk up to the mic. Anybody want to try giving me what they think is a shameless teasing bullet in your head? Give me a sizzling example of what you do to help people fix the problems in their lives based on results. Come on, somebody. Cowards. I'll choose somebody if you don't volunteer. There we go. It's off top of my head. Imagine winning the lottery with a $1 ticket. Winning the lottery with a $1 ticket. Not bad. What is your service or product that you're selling? How about a sure-fire way of winning the lottery with a $1 ticket? You have a product that will win the lottery? No. I'm just teasing. OK, so that's a good example. Thanks for doing that. That's too far into the blind stuff. Connect it back to what you're doing. Go ahead. What is your actual product or service? My actual product or service is window washing. However, I come out of the financial services business. So you went straight from Wall Street to the windows outside the building on Wall Street? I actually went from Wall Street to New Mexico to window washing, but that's a story for another day. OK. But I do subscribe to a number of investment newsletters still. OK. And I can tell you that investors who don't take action now are going to have their retirement plans crushed. There you go. Excellent. Go ahead. Good job. A powerful lead generation tactic that hardly anyone knows about or uses. Can you imagine saying that face to face to somebody? Now change it. Start with I've got and don't give me powerful lead generation. Give me another word. You're on the right track. I'm trying to get you down to a more conversational tone. People confuse good selling with hype all the time. This is one of my constant battles. You know I have headlines like the one leg of golfer and you know how does this old man golfer cripple by authorize still routinely humiliate PGA pros in the underground golf game. And you know they think that's hype. It can't be true. But the thing is I only operate from truth. Now the words I use may jack the truth up a bit. But it's all true. I'm not lying. I'm not I'm not exaggerating anything. I'm merely telling the story in a compelling way. So just try it one more time. I'm picking on you. So try it one more time. You know seriously just we're sitting in a bar. I don't know who you are and I'm ready to bolt because I think you're trying to sell me something. So rephrase it. I've got a way to double your lead generation that hardly anyone knows about. Okay. That's not bad. That's not bad. It's a good start. So I might say you know that might get my attention if my problem is lead generation. Right. So that's actually not bad. Keep it simple guys. I call it the one two punch of bullets. So if you said that then parentheses you may say how you would answer if I raised my eyebrows and you then furthered it. The other shoe drops. For lead generation for example if you're in a heavily if you're in a market niche with a lot of competition and what used to work isn't working anymore. You don't think about how to do things when you drive. You just do it automatically. But that's not what you do with a beginner. One of the things I like to do, and I just talked with my first, not my first, my first financial advisor that I actually like, I finally got one. But the reason I liked him is because we sat down, we actually had a conversation first. If you come away with, how many ideas do you need to come away with to be able to transform your business and your life from this weekend? How many ideas? Just one. You've probably been told that before. Just one can do it. Does 12 ideas make it 12 times more likely that that will happen? Absolutely not. One idea, it's all I ever came away with from school, from classes, from everything. My entire college career, I came away with one idea. Okay, go ahead. This is a short bullet point for, it's actually for a friend of mine who's in a karate business. It only takes, karate business, he owns a couple of karate schools, he's sitting back here and we were brainstorming a little bit. It only takes five words from a bully to crush your child's self-esteem. Okay, what are those five words? Oh, I don't know that. It's a blind bullet. This is a good little sub-lesson, not for Mike, but for everybody. If you're going to do that, you better have a good answer for that because in real life, what's going to happen when you do this kind of teasing? You better have a response. Either here are the five or here's three of them and the other two are two, I can't say it here because I might offend somebody, something that brings it forward. So give them half the information, keep the other two. Five is a good number? Not bad, Mike, not bad at all. Was that going this way or that way? Who's next? Okay, who's next? Okay, I'm speaking with a female client who's worried about her face. Either she's thinking about Botox or this $108 billion industry for cosmetosuticals and what I tell her is that I can take 10 years off your face in less than 10 days guaranteed. For the film, the five women in the back said they're in. Really women don't want rat poison injected into their pores? I don't do that. I have a system based on your skin type and it's called the exclusive European rejuvenated skin. You lost me right there. I probably lost her too. No, women will listen to this. You're absolutely on the right track. Find out where it hurts and go for it. That was good. That was a good example. Okay. In fact, as you guys listen to this, could you see that happening in real life in a bar? A guy talking to a woman in a bar, that's an extra... If your market is heavily gender split, then you got to be thinking about that. Most of the copyrights I deal with are men. I take special effort with the women because I believe that more women should be involved in as professional copyrights because the world just needs it because there's so many markets that are dominated by women. For the guys, they think they're going to go out there and do their stuff that works selling cars to get women to do this women-oriented stuff. What I do is I have the copywriters go and read a Daniel Steele novel, get every single magazine at the store that has a woman on the cover, Cos and Paul in a red book, all those scary things, and they have to go back and read them. Every single time those guys come back to me and they can't speak, they're stunned. They don't trust their wife anymore. They're scared to go to sleep at night now. The Daniel Steele novel especially. They start to understand how to talk to them. It's different. It's a little different. The joke among professional copyrighters is that men tend to talk about the specifics so much that you feel you could walk outside and construct the object yourself using just the blueprint that he has given you. Women tend to write in such emotional things that you feel really great but you have no idea what the product is. It's true. Or I even stand there for a while. We provide... What's your product? Fitness education in Australia. Fitness education in Australia as opposed to in New Zealand? Well, we're in New Zealand too. Go ahead. We educate you on how to become a personal trainer. No idea what you said. Say it again. We educate you on how to become a personal trainer. Boring knots. It just didn't jack me up. So let's see if we can help this out. I'm there and I say to Joe the bartender, I say, boy, I've had a bad day. I've got a personal training business in Sydney. And I think I'm going to go bust in about two months because I'm running out of ideas of how to bring people in. And you turn around and say, excuse me, I couldn't help overhearing. But talk. No, we're a college. So people come to us to study how to become personal trainer. In Australia, you have to actually do certificate courses. So it's like a college. So he would have already studied. My problem is you're spending so many hours writing up reports for clients and not having any time to do investigation. OK? And I'm drinking heavy. So what are you saying? So I was talking to this other PI, Sam, and he told me he was spending hours editing eight hours of surveillance video into five minutes and then didn't know what to do with it. So I uploaded to YouTube and called his client quickly and said, quick, look on YouTube to find that video. And then he pulled it down and he'd spend all night worrying that it was left up there forever and losing it confidentially and he didn't know what the heck to do. And I figured out a way that with ten minutes of just playing some of that surveillance video, you could capture it and embed it into a PDF document and share it with the client and they could play it in behind a secure firewall and no one would ever see it. Thus giving me an advantage over other guys who are probably exposing their clients down necessarily. Right. Good. That was good. That was good. I am a graphic designer and I would like to teach other graphic designers how to actually make money doing what they do. Most graphic designers... It's one of those... I was a graphic designer briefly, a colorblind graphic designer, I don't have enough time to tell the story. But I actually was working in Silicon Valley as a designer and it's one of those gigs where you are highly trained and poorly paid. That's right. So designers and artists tend to be very purists focusing on their skills but never making any money. So my headline would be learn five new ways to be an awesome graphic designer without learning any new design skills. And that would be... Not awesome. I wouldn't be. My problem is making money. So how to become the highest paid graphic artist in Nashville without one of the other graphic art skills. One of the things that I do is I have a lot of artist friends. I live near like... Not an artist colony but there's a lot of pockets of artists, some who are recognized, some who are not in Northern Nevada. And I become friends with these people because half of my head is in the art world anyway. And I always briefly bring up how to make money as an artist to them. 90% of them tell me what. Oh, no, no, no. Can't do that. I would spoil my purity or something. Better to be broke. And yet they're not broke. The reason we're talking about this is because they're complaining about being broke. And I tell them how not to be broke. I've told people how to make their galleries. I knew one guy who had three galleries closed in bankruptcy. And I said, but you know, in five minutes I can tell you how to make the gallery work. Now I don't want to hear it. But this would be removing the obstacles. Reversing the risk. When I read this thing, you'll understand that. Step nine, this is key. How many people have had a great conversation with somebody, maybe not in a bar? I know none of you ever go to bars and do this. But how many of you have had an actual conversation with somebody left and never heard from them again, even though you knew they were a hot prospect? A lot of people. That's what happens. He would frequently have authors on there or people who were actually businessmen or entrepreneurs. The Larry King effect would be an entrepreneur came on and would come off after a nice little chat with Larry King and they'd say, my God, I was in front of three million people talking to Larry King and they'd sit back and wait for the sales to come in. That was the best compliment I've heard. I am an idiot. I don't know anything more than you do. I'm not smarter than you. I have more experience. I spent 30 years on the front line trenches, but what did I do during those 30 years? Two things I did. One, what? I made every freaking mistake there is to make. I haven't been anywhere near the fairway. That was my life in advertising. But what did I do that made that worthwhile? I took notes and I learned my lessons. This is real world stuff. Am I a jerk? Absolutely not. I'm one of the sweetest guys you'll ever meet. I come across a little rough and I do make people cry during consultations, but I do it why? No, for their own good.
